category.text: $course$/Advanced Algebra (M.Sc. Mathematics)/CSIR PYQ — Unit V — Galois theory and solvability by radicals

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q1 — Gal(Q(√2)/Q)

questiontext.text: The Galois group \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})/\mathbb{Q})\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).
The extension has degree 2 and is Galois. The two automorphisms are the identity \(\mathrm{id}\) and the conjugation \(\sigma:\sqrt{2}\mapsto-\sqrt{2}\) (fixing \(\mathbb{Q}\)). These form a cyclic group of order 2, hence \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})/\mathbb{Q})\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q2 — Gal(Q(ζₙ)/Q)

questiontext.text: The Galois group \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q})\) where \(\zeta_n=e^{2\pi i/n}\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times\).
Each automorphism \(\sigma_k:\zeta_n\mapsto\zeta_n^k\) for \(\gcd(k,n)=1\) is well-defined and distinct. The map \(k\mapsto\sigma_k\) gives an isomorphism \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times\), a group of order \(\varphi(n)\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q3 — Gal(Q(2^{1/3},ω)/Q)

questiontext.text: The Galois group of \(x^3-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) (i.e., \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)/\mathbb{Q})\) where \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\)) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(S_3\).
The splitting field of \(x^3-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) has degree 6. The Galois group acts on the three roots \(\{\sqrt[3]{2},\omega\sqrt[3]{2},\omega^2\sqrt[3]{2}\}\) faithfully, embedding into \(S_3\). Since the group has order 6 and \(|S_3|=6\), we get \(\mathrm{Gal}\cong S_3\). The group is generated by \(\sigma:\sqrt[3]{2}\mapsto\omega\sqrt[3]{2},\,\omega\mapsto\omega\) (order 3) and \(\tau:\sqrt[3]{2}\mapsto\sqrt[3]{2},\,\omega\mapsto\omega^2\) (order 2).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q4 — Fundamental theorem of Galois theory

questiontext.text: The fundamental theorem of Galois theory establishes a bijective correspondence between:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Subgroups of \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) and intermediate fields between \(F\) and \(K\).
For a finite Galois extension \(K/F\) with \(G=\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\): (i) Subgroup \(H\le G\)\(\longleftrightarrow\) Fixed field \(K^H\). (ii) \([K^H:F]=[G:H]\) and \([K:K^H]=|H|\). (iii) \(H\trianglelefteq G\iff K^H/F\) is Galois. This is the Galois correspondence, order-reversing.

answer.text: Subgroups of \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) and intermediate fields between \(F\) and \(K\)
Prime ideals of \(F[x]\) and subfields of \(K\)
Polynomials over \(F\) and their roots in \(K\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q5 — [K:K^H] = |H|

questiontext.text: If \(K/F\) is a finite Galois extension with \(G=\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) and \(H\le G\), then \([K:K^H]\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(|H|\).
By the fundamental theorem of Galois theory, \([K:K^H]=|H|\) and \([K^H:F]=[G:H]=|G|/|H|\). These follow from Artin's theorem: if \(H\) is a finite group of automorphisms of \(K\), then \([K:K^H]=|H|\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q6 — Solvable by radicals

questiontext.text: A polynomial \(f(x)\in\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is solvable by radicals if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: The Galois group of \(f\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is a solvable group.
This is the central theorem of Galois theory. An equation is solvable by radicals iff its splitting field can be reached by a tower of radical extensions, which corresponds exactly to the Galois group being solvable. This theorem explained why degree \(\le 4\) equations are always solvable but degree 5 equations are not in general (since \(S_5\) is not solvable).

answer.text: The Galois group of \(f\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is solvable

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q7 — Abel–Ruffini theorem

questiontext.text: The Abel–Ruffini theorem states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: There is no general formula expressing roots of degree \(\ge 5\) polynomial equations by radicals.
Abel (1824) and Galois showed that the general polynomial equation of degree \(n\ge 5\) cannot be solved by radicals. This is because the Galois group of a generic degree-\(n\) polynomial is \(S_n\), and \(S_n\) is not solvable for \(n\ge 5\) (since \(A_n\) is simple non-abelian for \(n\ge 5\)).

answer.text: There is no general radical formula for roots of polynomials of degree \(\ge 5\)
Every polynomial has a root in \(\mathbb{C}\)
Every polynomial of degree \(\le 4\) is solvable by radicals
The Galois group of \(x^5-1\) is solvable

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q8 — Galois group of x⁴−2

questiontext.text: The Galois group of \(x^4-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(D_4\) (dihedral group of order 8).
The splitting field is \(\mathbb{Q}(2^{1/4},i)\) of degree 8 over \(\mathbb{Q}\). The Galois group has order 8 and is generated by \(\sigma:2^{1/4}\mapsto i\cdot 2^{1/4},\,i\mapsto i\) (order 4) and \(\tau:2^{1/4}\mapsto 2^{1/4},\,i\mapsto-i\) (order 2) with \(\tau\sigma\tau^{-1}=\sigma^{-1}\). This is \(D_4\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q9 — Normal subgroup and Galois correspondence

questiontext.text: Under the Galois correspondence, a subgroup \(H\trianglelefteq G=\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) corresponds to an intermediate field \(K^H\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(K^H/F\) is a Galois extension with \(\mathrm{Gal}(K^H/F)\cong G/H\).
When \(H\trianglelefteq G\), the fixed field \(K^H\) is not only an extension of \(F\) but a Galois extension. Moreover, restriction gives a surjection \(G\to\mathrm{Gal}(K^H/F)\) with kernel \(H\), so by the first isomorphism theorem \(\mathrm{Gal}(K^H/F)\cong G/H\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q10 — Gal(Fpⁿ/Fp)

questiontext.text: The Galois group \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p)\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) (cyclic of order \(n\)).
The extension \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p\) is Galois of degree \(n\). The Frobenius automorphism \(\phi:x\mapsto x^p\) has order \(n\) and generates the Galois group. Hence \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p)=\langle\phi\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q11 — Fixed field of Gal(K/F)

questiontext.text: The fixed field of the full Galois group \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) of a Galois extension \(K/F\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(F\).
By the fundamental theorem, the fixed field of \(G=\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) is \(K^G=F\) (elements of \(K\) fixed by every element of \(G\)). This is part of the defining property of a Galois extension: \(K^{\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)}=F\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q12 — |Gal(Q(√2,√3)/Q)|

questiontext.text: The order of \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})/\mathbb{Q})\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 4.
\([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]=4\) and the extension is Galois (splitting field of \((x^2-2)(x^2-3)\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\)). For a finite Galois extension, \(|\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)|=[K:F]\). The four automorphisms are determined by \((\sqrt{2}\mapsto\pm\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}\mapsto\pm\sqrt{3})\), forming \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q13 — Gal(Q(√2,√3)/Q) structure

questiontext.text: \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})/\mathbb{Q})\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) (Klein four-group).
The four automorphisms are \(\{\mathrm{id},\sigma,\tau,\sigma\tau\}\) where \(\sigma:\sqrt{2}\mapsto-\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}\mapsto\sqrt{3}\) and \(\tau:\sqrt{2}\mapsto\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}\mapsto-\sqrt{3}\). Each non-identity element has order 2, so the group is the Klein four-group \(V_4\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q14 — Galois closure of Q(2^{1/3})/Q

questiontext.text: The Galois closure of \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)\) where \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\).
The Galois closure is the splitting field of the minimal polynomial \(x^3-2\) of \(\sqrt[3]{2}\), which is \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega\sqrt[3]{2},\omega^2\sqrt[3]{2})=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)\). This has degree 6 over \(\mathbb{Q}\) with Galois group \(S_3\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q15 — Abelian extension

questiontext.text: A Galois extension \(K/F\) is called abelian if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) is an abelian group.
Examples: cyclotomic extensions \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}\) are abelian (Galois group \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times\)). The Kronecker–Weber theorem classifies all abelian extensions of \(\mathbb{Q}\): they are all subfields of cyclotomic fields \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)\) for some \(n\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q16 — Kronecker–Weber theorem

questiontext.text: The Kronecker–Weber theorem states that every abelian extension of \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Contained in a cyclotomic field \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)\) for some \(n\).
The Kronecker–Weber theorem (1853–1886) is a fundamental result of class field theory: any finite abelian extension of \(\mathbb{Q}\) is a subfield of some cyclotomic field \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)\). In particular, every abelian extension of \(\mathbb{Q}\) can be generated by roots of unity.

answer.text: Contained in a cyclotomic field \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)\) for some \(n\)
Generated by a single rational number

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q17 — Discriminant of polynomial

questiontext.text: The discriminant \(\Delta\) of a polynomial \(f(x)\) over a field \(F\) satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\Delta=0\) if and only if \(f\) has a repeated root.
The discriminant \(\Delta(f)=\prod_{i

answer.text: \(\Delta=0\) if and only if \(f\) has a repeated root
\(\Delta\) is always a positive real number
\(\Delta\) equals the product of the roots

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q18 — Irreducible polynomial of prime degree with 2 non-real roots

questiontext.text: If \(f(x)\in\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is irreducible of prime degree \(p\) with exactly 2 non-real roots, then the Galois group of \(f\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(S_p\).
By a theorem: if \(f\in\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is irreducible of prime degree \(p\) and has exactly 2 non-real complex roots, then \(\mathrm{Gal}(f/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_p\). The proof uses that the Galois group contains a \(p\)-cycle (from irreducibility) and a transposition (from complex conjugation swapping the 2 non-real roots), and these generate \(S_p\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q19 — Galois group as subgroup of Sₙ

questiontext.text: The Galois group of a polynomial \(f(x)\) of degree \(n\) over \(F\) can be viewed as a subgroup of \(S_n\) because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Each automorphism in \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) permutes the \(n\) roots of \(f\).
If \(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_n\) are the roots of \(f\) in the splitting field \(K\), then every \(\sigma\in\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) maps roots to roots (since \(f\) has coefficients in \(F\) which \(\sigma\) fixes). This gives an injective homomorphism \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\hookrightarrow S_n\), making \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) a transitive subgroup of \(S_n\) when \(f\) is irreducible.

answer.text: Each automorphism permutes the \(n\) roots of \(f\)
The Galois group always has order \(n!\)
There is a bijection between \(F\) and \(S_n\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q20 — A₅ not solvable implies degree 5 not solvable

questiontext.text: The general polynomial equation of degree 5 over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is not solvable by radicals because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: The symmetric group \(S_5\) is not solvable (since \(A_5\) is simple non-abelian).
For a generic degree-5 polynomial, the Galois group is \(S_5\). The derived series of \(S_5\) is \(S_5\supset A_5\supset A_5\supset\cdots\) (stationary, since \([A_5,A_5]=A_5\)). Since the derived series doesn't reach \(\{e\}\), \(S_5\) is not solvable, and by Galois theory, the equation is not solvable by radicals.

answer.text: The symmetric group \(S_5\) is not solvable (since \(A_5\) is simple non-abelian)
Polynomials of degree 5 have no roots in \(\mathbb{C}\)
The quadratic formula cannot be extended to degree 5
Degree 5 polynomials are always irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q21 — Kummer extension

questiontext.text: A Kummer extension of degree \(n\) over a field \(F\) (containing a primitive \(n\)-th root of unity) is a Galois extension whose Galois group is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: A subgroup of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) (i.e., a cyclic group of order dividing \(n\)).
A Kummer extension is of the form \(F(\sqrt[n]{a})/F\) where \(F\) contains a primitive \(n\)-th root of unity. The Galois group is cyclic: \(\sigma:\sqrt[n]{a}\mapsto\zeta\sqrt[n]{a}\) for a primitive \(n\)-th root \(\zeta\). If \(x^n-a\) is irreducible, the group is \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q22 — Galois extension: definition

questiontext.text: A finite extension \(K/F\) is Galois if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(K/F\) is normal and separable.
Equivalently: \(|\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)|=[K:F]\), or \(K\) is the splitting field of a separable polynomial over \(F\). In characteristic 0, every normal extension is automatically separable. Over finite fields, every finite extension is separable (since finite fields are perfect).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q23 — Gal(Q(ζ₅)/Q)

questiontext.text: The Galois group \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})\) where \(\zeta_5=e^{2\pi i/5}\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\).
\([\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5):\mathbb{Q}]=\varphi(5)=4\). The group \((\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z})^\times=\{1,2,3,4\}\) is cyclic of order 4 (generated by 2, since \(2^1=2,2^2=4,2^3=3,2^4=1\)). So \(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q24 — Number of intermediate fields of Q(ζ₅)/Q

questiontext.text: The number of intermediate fields between \(\mathbb{Q}\) and \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)\) (including \(\mathbb{Q}\) and \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)\) themselves) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 4.
\(\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) has subgroups \(\{0\}, 2\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}, \mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) — exactly 3 subgroups. By the Galois correspondence, there are 3 intermediate fields \(\mathbb{Q}\subsetneq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5+\zeta_5^{-1})\subsetneq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)\)... wait, that gives 3 intermediate fields. Including both endpoints: the subgroups of \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) are \(\{0\},\langle 2\rangle,\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) — 3 subgroups — corresponding to 3 intermediate fields. So the answer is 3.

Correction: The subgroups of \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) are \(\{0\}\), \(\{0,2\}\), and \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\), which are 3 subgroups. These correspond to 3 intermediate fields: \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)\), \(\mathbb{Q}(\cos(2\pi/5))\), and \(\mathbb{Q}\). So there are 3 intermediate fields.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit V Q25 — Cyclotomic polynomial Φₙ(x)

questiontext.text: For any positive integer \(n\), the cyclotomic polynomial \(\Phi_n(x)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
\(\Phi_n(x)=\prod_{\substack{1\le k\le n\\\gcd(k,n)=1}}(x-\zeta_n^k)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]\) where \(\zeta_n=e^{2\pi i/n}\). It has degree \(\varphi(n)\) and is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) (Gauss). This is equivalent to saying that all primitive \(n\)-th roots of unity are conjugate over \(\mathbb{Q}\), or that \([\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n):\mathbb{Q}]=\varphi(n)\).
